title: The Fixed Classroom — Your First Array
instruction:
In Chapter 5, you used loops to process scores. But where were those scores *stored*? It's time to talk about **arrays**.

An array is like a **classroom with a fixed number of seats**. You decide the size when you create it, and it never changes. A classroom with 5 seats:

```go
var seats [5]string
```

This creates 5 empty string slots. Declare an array `seats` of 5 strings and print it — you'll see the zero values (empty strings).
hint: var seats [5]string — then fmt.Println(seats). You'll see [    ] since strings start as empty.

title: Assign Students to Seats
instruction:
Array **indices start at 0**, not 1. The first seat is `seats[0]`, the second is `seats[1]`, and so on.

Assign these students to the classroom:
- Seat 0: `"Alice"`
- Seat 1: `"Bob"`
- Seat 2: `"Carol"`
- Seat 3: `"David"`
- Seat 4: `"Eve"`

Then print the whole array.
hint: seats[0] = "Alice" — repeat for each seat, then fmt.Println(seats).

title: Who's First? Who's Last?
instruction:
You can access any individual seat using its index. The first seat is `seats[0]`. The last seat (in a 5-element array) is `seats[4]` — or more robustly, `seats[len(seats)-1]`.

Print the **first** student, then the **last** student on separate lines.
hint: fmt.Println(seats[0]) for the first. fmt.Println(seats[len(seats)-1]) for the last.

title: Print Every Student
instruction: Use the loop skills from Chapter 5 to print every student in the classroom — one per line. Loop from index `0` to `len(seats)-1`.
hint: for i := 0; i < len(seats); i++ { fmt.Println(seats[i]) }

title: Total the Exam Scores
instruction:
Each of the 5 students has an exam score. Sum all the scores and print the total.

This combines your loop skills (Chapter 5) with array indexing — a very common pattern you'll use constantly.
hint: sum := 0. Then loop: sum += scores[i]. Print sum after the loop.

title: Who Sits Here? — Searching an Array
instruction: The principal asks: "Is Carol in the classroom?" Loop through `seats` and print `"Found Carol"` if her name appears, or `"Carol not found"` if it doesn't. Use the `break` from Chapter 5 to stop once you find her.
hint: Loop and check if seats[i] == "Carol". If found, print "Found Carol" and break. After the loop, if never found, print "Carol not found".

title: Swap Two Seats
instruction:
Bob and David want to swap seats. To swap two values in an array, you need a **temporary variable** to hold one value while you move the other:

```
temp = a
a = b
b = temp
```

Swap seats 1 (Bob) and 3 (David), then print the whole array.
hint: temp := seats[1]; seats[1] = seats[3]; seats[3] = temp — then fmt.Println(seats).
